generatedNote: Physician-reviewed prototype. All medical content is a close paraphrase of the cited authoritative source. No commercial interaction databases used.

records.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
records.potentialConsequence: Carbamazepine is a hepatic enzyme-inducing drug. Enzyme-inducing drugs can increase clearance of contraceptive hormones and could reduce the contraceptive effectiveness of combined hormonal contraception. The guidance advises that combined hormonal contraception is not recommended during use of the enzyme inducer and for 28 days after stopping it.
records.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
records.population: People using or considering combined hormonal contraception who take an enzyme-inducing drug
records.sourceTitle: FSRH CEU Guidance: Drug Interactions with Hormonal Contraception (May 2022)
records.sourceOrganization: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare (FSRH)
records.sourceSection: Section 4, Quick reference guide: enzyme-inducing drugs and contraception — CHC row; Section 3.1.1
records.limitations: Carbamazepine is treated as a representative hepatic enzyme inducer. The magnitude of any reduction in contraceptive effectiveness for an individual is not established. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited source and individual clinical context.
records.machineMatchingNotes: hormonalConceptIds includes estrogen_containing_oral_contraceptive because the record's own synonym list covers ethinylestradiol-containing (estrogen-containing) products. Derived mapping; flagged for physician review.

records.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
records.potentialConsequence: Rifampicin and rifabutin are described as potent hepatic enzyme inducers. Enzyme induction increases clearance of contraceptive hormones and could reduce the contraceptive effectiveness of combined hormonal contraception. The guidance advises use of an alternative effective method that is not affected by enzyme induction; the two-pill approach described for other enzyme inducers is explicitly excepted for the potent inducers rifampicin/rifabutin. The US Medical Eligibility Criteria classify combined hormonal contraceptives as category 3 with rifampin or rifabutin therapy.
records.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
records.evidenceLevel: Clinical guideline (UK national guidance and US Medical Eligibility Criteria)
records.population: People using or considering combined hormonal contraception who take rifampicin or rifabutin
records.sourceTitle: FSRH CEU Guidance: Drug Interactions with Hormonal Contraception (May 2022); U.S. Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use, 2024
records.sourceOrganization: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare (FSRH); U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
records.sourceSection: FSRH Section 4, CHC quick reference (rifampicin/rifabutin exception); CDC US-MEC 2024 Summary of Classifications, Antimicrobial therapy — Rifampin or rifabutin therapy (CHC = 3)
records.limitations: Two national sources are combined for this record. The CDC classification page (https://www.cdc.gov/contraception/hcp/usspr/classifications-mec-contraception.html) should be confirmed alongside the FSRH guidance. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited sources and individual clinical context.
records.machineMatchingNotes: Multi-member record: matches when the patient medication is any explicit member (rifampicin, rifabutin). hormonalConceptIds derivation as in INT-001.

records.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
records.potentialConsequence: The guidance states that enzyme-inducing drugs could reduce contraceptive effectiveness of all progestogen-only pills. It advises that the progestogen-only pill is not recommended during use of the enzyme inducer and for 28 days after stopping it, and that using two progestogen-only pills together is not recommended in this situation.
records.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
records.population: People using or considering a progestogen-only pill who take an enzyme-inducing drug
records.sourceTitle: FSRH CEU Guidance: Drug Interactions with Hormonal Contraception (May 2022)
records.sourceOrganization: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare (FSRH)
records.sourceSection: Section 4, Quick reference guide (continued) — POP row; Section 3.1.1 Key information
records.limitations: The interacting medication is a drug class (hepatic enzyme inducers) rather than a single agent. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited source and individual clinical context.
records.machineMatchingNotes: Closed-class membership is exactly the single agents already enumerated in this record's synonym list. Class words (e.g. 'enzyme inducer') never match by themselves. Members are not extended beyond the record's own enumeration; flagged for physician review.

records.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
records.potentialConsequence: The guidance states that enzyme-inducing drugs could reduce contraceptive effectiveness of the etonogestrel implant. It advises that the implant is not recommended during use of the enzyme inducer and for 28 days after stopping it, that an alternative effective method should be recommended, and that using two implants together is not recommended.
records.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
records.population: People using or considering the etonogestrel implant who take an enzyme-inducing drug
records.sourceTitle: FSRH CEU Guidance: Drug Interactions with Hormonal Contraception (May 2022)
records.sourceOrganization: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare (FSRH)
records.sourceSection: Section 4, Quick reference guide (continued) — ENG-IMP row; Section 3.1.1 Key information
records.limitations: The interacting medication is a drug class (hepatic enzyme inducers) rather than a single agent. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited source and individual clinical context.
records.machineMatchingNotes: Closed-class membership is exactly the single agents already enumerated in this record's synonym list. Class words never match by themselves.

records.interactionDirection: Contraceptive hormone may reduce exposure to the interacting medication (reversed direction)
records.potentialConsequence: This interaction runs in the reverse direction to the enzyme-inducer records. Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives induce glucuronidation of lamotrigine and decrease lamotrigine concentrations by approximately 50%, which could reduce seizure control while the contraceptive is taken; during any hormone-free (pill-free) interval lamotrigine levels are expected to rise. The FDA label states that dosage adjustments will be necessary in most patients who start or stop estrogen-containing oral contraceptives while taking lamotrigine.
records.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
records.evidenceLevel: Product label (FDA prescribing information) and clinical guideline
records.population: People taking lamotrigine who use or are considering an estrogen-containing oral contraceptive
records.sourceTitle: LAMICTAL (lamotrigine) Prescribing Information, Revised 10/2025; FSRH CEU Guidance: Drug Interactions with Hormonal Contraception (May 2022)
records.sourceOrganization: U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA); Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare (FSRH)
records.sourceSection: FDA label Highlights, Drug Interactions and Sections 7/12.3 ('Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives decrease lamotrigine concentrations by approximately 50%') and Section 5.9; FSRH Section 3.1.3 and Section 9 lamotrigine FAQ
records.limitations: REVERSED DIRECTION relative to the other records: the concern is reduced lamotrigine exposure (seizure-control risk), not reduced contraceptive efficacy. The record deliberately carries no dose recommendation. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited sources and individual clinical context.
records.machineMatchingNotes: REVERSED direction: the contraceptive reduces lamotrigine exposure. hormonalConceptIds includes combined_hormonal_contraceptive because the record's own synonym list covers combined oral contraceptives (estrogen-containing). Derived mapping; flagged for physician review.

records.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
records.potentialConsequence: Medicines or herbal remedies that induce CYP3A4 enzymes increase the metabolism of levonorgestrel and reduce its plasma levels, which may reduce the contraceptive efficacy of levonorgestrel-containing emergency contraception. Elevated enzyme levels can persist for up to 4 weeks after the enzyme-inducing medicine is stopped.
records.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
records.population: People seeking levonorgestrel emergency contraception who have used a hepatic enzyme inducer within the last 4 weeks
records.sourceTitle: Drug Safety Update: Levonorgestrel-containing emergency hormonal contraception — advice on interactions with hepatic enzyme inducers and contraceptive efficacy (September 2016)
records.sourceOrganization: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA)
records.sourceSection: Sections 'Effect of hepatic enzyme inducers on levonorgestrel metabolism' and 'Examples of enzyme inducers that reduce plasma levonorgestrel levels'
records.limitations: The source also carries dose and alternative-method advice (e.g. copper IUD, doubling the levonorgestrel dose); that directive content is deliberately excluded here to keep potentialConsequence non-directive. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited source and individual clinical context.
records.machineMatchingNotes: Closed-class membership is exactly the single agents enumerated in this record's synonym list. The class word 'barbiturates' is NOT expanded to members (phenobarbital is deliberately absent here because this record's list does not name it); pending physician enumeration.

changes: Added machine-matching metadata per task spec §22: matchType, hormonalConceptIds, interactingConceptIds (explicit closed-class membership), interactionDirectionCode, machineMatchingNotes.
Removed the bare alias 'the pill' from hormonalSynonyms (INT-001, INT-002, INT-005); it is now an ambiguous hormonal reference handled by abstention per task spec §15.7.
No medical prose field (interactionDirection, potentialConsequence, clinicianConsideration, evidenceLevel, population, source*, jurisdiction, limitations) was altered.
physicianVerified remains false on all records: sign-off is pending and is the physician's claim to make. Runtime eligibility is computed at load time and never stored.
